channel.title: NYT > U.S. News
channel.copyright: Copyright 2026 The New York Times Company

item.title: China Has Arrested U.S. Scholar Who Studies Myanmar Politics
item.description: The arrest of U Min Zin, a graduate student at U.C. Berkeley and founder of a research group in Myanmar, took place soon after President Trump met with Xi Jinping in China.
item.dc:creator: Edward Wong
item.media:credit: J. Scott Applewhite/Associated Press

item.title: Can Democrats Overcome G.O.P. Gerrymandering?
item.description: Our chief political analyst, Nate Cohn, who writes The Tilt newsletter, looks at the Republicans’ advantage in the House of Representatives after partisan redistricting. To win the House, how much of the popular vote would Democrats need to win?
item.dc:creator: Nate Cohn, Laura Bult, June Kim, Edward Vega and Pierre Kattar

item.title: Trump Suspends Funding for Los Angeles Homeless Agency
item.description: The Trump administration cited misspending among the reasons for blocking funds to the Los Angeles Homeless Services Authority. Mayor Karen Bass warned that “people will lose their lives.”
item.dc:creator: Jill Cowan
item.media:credit: Kenny Holston/The New York Times
item.media:description: Scott Turner, the housing secretary, said his agency would not fund what he called the “corrupt failure” of Los Angeles’s homelessness efforts.

item.title: Supreme Court Blocks Alabama From Executing Inmate Using Nitrogen Gas
item.description: The unsigned decision for now spares Jeffery Lee, a convicted murderer, and could lead to a broader fight over the relatively new execution method.
item.dc:creator: Rick Rojas and Abbie VanSickle
item.media:credit: Kenny Holston/The New York Times
item.media:description: Jeffery Lee was scheduled for execution on Thursday before the ruling.

item.title: What to Know About Jay Clayton, Trump’s Pick for Intelligence Director
item.description: Mr. Clayton, the U.S. attorney in Manhattan and a former chairman of the Securities and Exchange Commission, has been overseeing an office known for prominent cases.
item.dc:creator: Benjamin Weiser
item.media:credit: Jefferson Siegel for The New York Times
item.media:description: Jay Clayton, then serving as the U.S. attorney, at Gracie Mansion in New York last March. President Trump said he plans to choose him as the next director of national intelligence.

item.title: National Mall Grass Vandalized with ’86 47’ Stains
item.description: The vandalism was the latest in a spate of apparent demonstrations involving the slang term, which the Trump administration says is a reference to assassinating the president.
item.dc:creator: Neil Vigdor
item.media:credit: Nathan Howard/Reuters
item.media:description: The National Park Service began a criminal investigation after discovering the numbers stained into the grass near the Washington Monument.

item.title: Melania Trump Unveils New Investment Accounts for Foster Children
item.description: The program allows states to invest benefits they receive on behalf of foster children in their care.
item.dc:creator: Erica L. Green
item.media:credit: Allison Robbert/Associated Press
item.media:description: Under the new program announced by Melania Trump, the Treasury Department will allow states to act as legal guardians to open accounts for foster youth in their care.

item.title: Some States Opt Out of Trump’s ‘Great American State Fair’
item.description: At least five have declined to participate, the latest sign that the national 250th birthday celebration has become a fragmented and partisan affair as the president seeks his imprint.
item.dc:creator: Eileen Sullivan
item.media:credit: Al Drago for The New York Times
item.media:description: Construction on the National Mall in preparation for the Great American State Fair.

item.title: Children’s Lemonade Stand Is Robbed in Broad Daylight in Boston
item.description: The police were searching for two boys who they said flashed a gun at a girl, 11, and boy, 12, who were running the stand in South Boston on Wednesday.
item.dc:creator: Mack Liederman

item.title: Gabbard Revokes Biden-Era Assessments on Mysterious Ailments
item.description: Earlier reviews had cast doubt on the idea that a foreign adversary was behind Havana syndrome, a range of symptoms that American spies and diplomats reported.
item.dc:creator: Julian E. Barnes
item.media:credit: Kenny Holston/The New York Times
item.media:description: Actions taken by Tulsi Gabbard, the director of national intelligence, opened up the possibility that the Trump administration would take a new look at whether Russia or another power could be behind Havana syndrome.

item.title: Workers at Washington Paper Mill Died of Chemical Burns
item.description: The coroner of Cowlitz County confirmed the cause of death of 10 of the 11 workers who perished last month in the worst industrial accident in the state’s modern history.
item.dc:creator: Anna Griffin
item.media:credit: David Ryder/Reuters
item.media:description: The Nippon Dynawave Packaging pulp and paper mill in Longview, Wash., after the deadly accident.

item.title: Body of 5-Year-Old Girl Swept Away in California Is Found
item.description: The girl was with her mother and a sibling near the shore in Laguna Beach, Calif., on Tuesday when she was pulled into the surf. Authorities had called off the search on Wednesday.
item.dc:creator: Ishani Desai
item.media:credit: Mindy Schauer/The Orange County Register, via Associated Press
item.media:description: A lifeguard rescue boat patrolled the coastline in Laguna Beach, Calif., on Wednesday during the search for the 5-year-old girl who had been swept away.

item.title: 2026 World Cup: Hidden FIFA Costs & a USMNT Preview
item.description: And, our top three stories as the tournament begins.
item.dc:creator: Tamerra Griffin, James McNicholas, Hannah Vanbiber, Adam Crafton and Pablo Torre
item.media:credit: Cesar Rodriguez for The New York Times

item.title: Postal Service Issues Proposal to Block Mail Ballots in States That Don’t Turn Over Data
item.description: Democrats and voting-rights groups have challenged the proposed rule as a harmful federal intrusion that could affect millions of voters who cast their ballots by mail.
item.dc:creator: Adam Sella and Nick Corasaniti
item.media:credit: Jeff Chiu/Associated Press
item.media:description: Department of Elections workers sort mail-in ballots for the California primary election at City Hall in San Francisco, Calif., earlier this month.

item.title: Trump Picks Jay Clayton for Director of National Intelligence After Backlash Over Bill Pulte
item.description: The president said he would nominate Jay Clayton, the U.S. attorney in Manhattan and the former chairman of the Securities and Exchange Commission, for the permanent role.
item.dc:creator: Julian E. Barnes, Jonah E. Bromwich, Devlin Barrett and Benjamin Weiser
item.category: Office of the Director of National Intelligence
item.media:credit: Dave Sanders for The New York Times
item.media:description: President Trump’s pick to be the director of national intelligence, Jay Clayton, took over as the U.S. attorney for Manhattan in April 2025.

item.title: Trump Unveils UFC ‘Claw’ Ahead of Cage Match at the White House
item.description: The towering claw will be the site of an Ultimate Fighting Championship cage match on Sunday, which is President Trump’s 80th birthday.
item.dc:creator: Shawn McCreesh
item.media:credit: Haiyun Jiang/The New York Times

item.title: Avril Haines, Former Intelligence Chief, Will Lead Carnegie Endowment for International Peace
item.description: In the run-up to the invasion of Ukraine, Ms. Haines helped devise the strategy of declassifying evidence of Russia’s military buildup in an effort to rally European allies.
item.dc:creator: David E. Sanger
item.media:credit: Kent Nishimura for The New York Times
item.media:description: Avril D. Haines will lead one of Washington’s premier think tanks, founded in 1910 by Andrew Carnegie “to hasten the abolition of international war.”

item.title: After Senate Loss, Cornyn Predicts ‘Miserable’ Final Two Years for Trump
item.description: In his first extensive interview since his defeat by a Trump-backed challenger, the Texas Republican said the Senate was in for a “bumpy ride” as he and others flex new political freedom.
item.dc:creator: Carl Hulse
item.media:credit: Pete Marovich for The New York Times
item.media:description: “There’s never going to be good enough for him, other than 100 percent, you know, slavish adherence to whatever he wants,” Senator John Cornyn said of President Trump. “But obviously that’s not what the senator’s role is supposed to be.”

item.title: More Trump Losses in Court and a Federal Judge in Hot Water
item.description: A roundup of legal news from the last week.
item.media:credit: Haiyun Jiang/The New York Times
item.media:description: The United States Supreme Court.

item.title: Dallas Takes Step Toward Leaving City Hall Building
item.description: City leaders rejected a plan to repair the aging building, moving the city closer to opening a prime downtown site for redevelopment.
item.dc:creator: Jesus Jiménez
item.media:credit: Desiree Rios for The New York Times
item.media:description: The interior of Dallas City Hall, a building designed by the architect I.M. Pei.

item.title: Vance Boelter Pleads Guilty in Assassination of Minnesota Lawmaker
item.description: Federal prosecutors said they would not seek the death penalty for Vance Boelter, who still faces charges in state court.
item.dc:creator: Mitch Smith
item.media:credit: Alex Kormann/Minnesota Star Tribune, via Associated Press
item.media:description: Daniel N. Rosen, the top federal prosecutor in Minnesota, spoke at a news conference.

item.title: Justice Dept. Pushes Limits of Its Power Over State Elections
item.description: The department once tried to stay out of state elections, urging caution. It is now pressing forward with claims of fraud as President Trump revives his unfounded assertions that elections cannot be trusted.
item.dc:creator: Devlin Barrett
item.media:credit: Philip Cheung for The New York Times
item.media:description: Voters casting their ballots in California’s primary races in Los Angeles last week.

item.title: House Rejects Bill to Extend Surveillance Power With FISA Section 702 Set to Expire
item.description: A measure to temporarily continue a key surveillance law failed in the House, and members left for a weeklong recess, making an expiration all but inevitable.
item.dc:creator: Robert Jimison and Dustin Volz
item.media:credit: Salwan Georges for The New York Times
item.media:description: “We cannot allow that to go dark,” Speaker Mike Johnson told reporters on Wednesday as prospects for the law being renewed appeared increasingly grim.

item.title: Trump and Hegseth Broadcast U.S. Military Strikes Before They Happen
item.description: Commanders do not usually speak publicly about future operations to avoid jeopardizing the mission’s success.
item.dc:creator: Eric Schmitt
item.category: US and Israeli Attack on Iran (2026)
United States Defense and Military Forces
item.media:credit: Doug Mills/The New York Times
item.media:description: President Trump said he was telegraphing the American strikes to pressure Iran to reach a deal to open the Strait of Hormuz.

item.title: In a Trump-Endorsed Presidential Candidate, Colombia’s Women See a Familiar Far-Right Playbook
item.description: Colombia’s presidential front-runner has sparked a debate about masculinity and machismo. Women’s rights groups see a familiar right-wing playbook.
item.dc:creator: Annie Correal
item.media:credit: Federico Rios for The New York Times
item.media:description: The presidential candidate Abelardo De La Espriella during an election rally last month in Medellín, Colombia.

item.title: Sex, Lies and Secrets: A Federal Judge’s Trysts Go Public
item.description: Now, Judge Eleanor Ross’s career and caseload are under scrutiny. And her punishment, a private reprimand, has sparked backlash.
item.dc:creator: Nicholas Bogel-Burroughs and Mattathias Schwartz
item.media:credit: C-SPAN
item.media:description: Judge Eleanor Ross during a congressional hearing in 2014.

item.title: These Young Politicians Want to Fix America’s Housing Problems
item.description: A cohort of young Millennial and Gen Z politicians have centered their campaigns this year on housing costs, and the divide is more generational than partisan.
item.dc:creator: Kate Zernike
item.media:credit: Sophie Park for The New York Times
item.media:description: David Morales, 27, a candidate for mayor of Providence, R.I., has made housing central to his pitch for “a city all our neighbors can afford.”

item.title: Conor McGregor’s Comeback: A Tale of Banned Drugs and a Famous Doctor
item.description: McGregor, the Ultimate Fighting Championship’s main attraction, had the support of the prominent sports physician Neal ElAttrache when he decided to use performance-enhancing drugs.
item.dc:creator: Michael S. Schmidt
item.media:credit: John Locher/Associated Press
item.media:description: Conor McGregor preparing to fight Dustin Poirier in Las Vegas in 2021. McGregor suffered a badly broken leg during the bout.
